instructions: Build a complete HTML page. Give it a title in the <head> and an <h1> heading in the <body> that says 'Hello, World!'
starter:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title>My Page</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your h1 here -->
  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Has a <title> tag in the head

hints:
An h1 tag looks like this:

Your text here

— open tag, content, close tag.
Put your h1 inside the <body> tag. The body is where everything visible goes.
Make sure the text is exactly: Hello, World! — capital H, capital W, comma, exclamation mark.

steps.title: You started with DOCTYPE
steps.explanation: The very first line you wrote declares this as an HTML5 document. Browsers need this to render your page correctly — without it they fall into a broken legacy mode.
steps.why: Always write this on line 1, with no spaces before it.

steps.title: The root <html> element
steps.explanation: Your entire page lives inside <html>. It opens at the top and closes at the very bottom — it's the container for everything.

steps.title: The <head> — invisible metadata
steps.explanation: Everything in <head> is invisible to the user. It holds information about the page: the title that shows in the tab, links to stylesheets, and other metadata.

steps.title: You gave the page a title
steps.explanation: Your <title> text appears in the browser tab and in search engine results. It's not the heading on the page — it's the label for the page itself.
steps.why: A good, specific title improves SEO and helps users know what tab they're looking at.

steps.title: The <body> — everything visible
steps.explanation: Everything a user sees on the page lives inside <body>. Text, images, buttons, links — it all goes here.

steps.title: Your <h1> heading
steps.explanation: You wrote the most important heading on the page. <h1> is the top-level heading — browsers render it large and bold by default, and search engines treat it as the main topic of your page.
steps.why: One <h1> per page. It tells both humans and search engines: this is what this page is about.
